description: Project Apex is a local-first autonomous agent that executes real work on the host machine instead of returning instructions for humans to execute. The system is designed for power users managing development environments, containers, and local automation flows. Apex runs with direct system access under a strict policy firewall, produces complete audit trails, and keeps operational data local by default.

userStories.title: Native Shell Execution Engine
userStories.description: Implement a robust shell execution wrapper with support for bash/zsh/powershell, incremental output streaming, user cancellation, and bounded timeouts.
acceptanceCriteria: Supports bash, zsh, and powershell as selectable shell types.
Output streams incrementally via child_process.spawn (not exec); consumers receive chunks as they arrive.
User can cancel any running command at any time; the process is killed and cancellation is recorded.
Default force-timeout is 15 minutes (900s); policy can extend per-command.
Each execution records its PID and output ref in local state for crash recovery use by JAL-007.
Command injection check: reject shell metacharacters in untrusted input before execution.
sudo execution is blocked at this layer (deferred to Tier 3 policy in JAL-003).
userStories.technicalNotes: Use child_process.spawn for streaming. Record PIDs and output refs in local state for cancellation and crash recovery. ExecOptions and ExecResult types are defined in src/apex/types/index.ts — use them.
safetyGates: Command injection check on all inputs
sudo blocked unconditionally at execution layer

userStories.title: Docker Lifecycle Integration
userStories.description: Add native Docker tools for listing, starting, stopping, building, and inspecting local containers. All Docker operations flow through the policy firewall.
acceptanceCriteria: Supports list, start, stop, build, and inspect operations against the local Docker daemon.
Output streams incrementally to callers (same streaming contract as JAL-001 shell execution).
User can cancel any in-flight Docker operation; the operation is terminated and logged.
Default force-timeout is 15 minutes unless policy extends it.
Docker socket or group access is preferred; privileged execution is blocked by default and requires an explicit user-approved policy exception.
Every Docker operation is classified by the policy firewall (JAL-003) before execution; destructive ops (docker prune, rm) require Tier 2 approval.
Docker privilege model violations are audit-logged with action, tier, and reason.
userStories.technicalNotes: Use Docker CLI via child_process.spawn (streaming) or dockerode SDK. Privilege checks are delegated to the JAL-003 policy firewall — do not re-implement tier logic here. Prefer docker socket/group access over --privileged.
safetyGates: Privileged mode blocked by default
docker prune and rm classified as Tier 2 destructive ops

userStories.title: Tiered Firewall & Policy Implementation
userStories.description: Implement the deterministic policy firewall that classifies every action into a tier and enforces approval rules before execution proceeds.
acceptanceCriteria: Tier 1 (auto-approve): read-only commands; package operations against the allowlist at ~/.apex/policy/package-allowlist.json.
Tier 2 (HITL required): destructive operations (rm, docker prune, chmod); non-trivial file modifications; system-level changes; SSH task execution.
Tier 3 (blocked by default): sudo execution; network configuration changes; user and permission manipulation.
Every executed action has a tier classification and a TierDecision record written to the audit log before execution.
Tier 2 actions pause execution and emit an approval request; execution resumes only after explicit approval is received.
Tier 3 actions are rejected immediately with a structured error and an audit log entry; no bypass path exists.
The package allowlist at ~/.apex/policy/package-allowlist.json is versioned, user-editable, and every change is audit-logged.
Policy enforcement is a wrapper layer: execution engines (JAL-001, JAL-002) call the firewall before running any command.
userStories.technicalNotes: Firewall wraps both the shell execution engine (JAL-001) and Docker integration (JAL-002). Policy files live under ~/.apex/policy/. All policy file changes must be audit-logged. Approval tokens for Tier 2 are single-use and short-lived.
safetyGates: Allowlist validation before Tier 1 package ops
Approval token integrity check before Tier 2 execution
Zero Tier 3 bypass paths

userStories.title: Policy-Bounded File Operations
userStories.description: Implement file operation controls that enforce Tier 1/2 boundaries based on workspace roots and operation type.
acceptanceCriteria: Tier 1 (auto-approved): create and edit files inside configured APEX_WORKSPACE_ROOTS.
Tier 2 (HITL required): recursive deletes (rm -r), chmod/chown, writes outside workspace roots, shell profile edits (.bashrc, .zshrc, etc.), and system file edits (/etc/, /usr/, etc.).
Workspace roots are configurable in ~/.apex/policy/workspace-roots.json and changes are audit-logged.
Operations outside policy boundaries are rejected with a structured error and an audit log entry.
File operation decisions emit TierDecision records to the audit log with action, tier, and reason fields.
userStories.technicalNotes: Wrap all file I/O through a PolicyFileOps class that checks paths against workspace roots before execution. Path resolution must be absolute (resolve symlinks) before boundary checks. See PRD §5.2 for full governance model.
safetyGates: Path traversal check (reject ../ escapes)
Symlink resolution before boundary check
Audit log entry on every rejection

userStories.title: Integrated Model Switching & Auth Flow
userStories.description: Provide a provider-agnostic login and model switching flow using the shared config GUI, with secure OS-backed credential handling and deterministic fallback.
acceptanceCriteria: Users log in without API key entry in the standard flow; auth uses OAuth, device-code, session auth, or CLI hooks.
Model configuration uses the shared config GUI component at /home/spoq/ai-vision/tools/config-gui; if unavailable, fall back to a bundled equivalent and show a non-fatal warning.
CLI hook contract: `apex auth login --provider <name> --json` writes `{ status, provider, expires_at, message }` to stdout and exits 0 on success or 1 on failure.
Provider and model switches require no code changes; the provider-agnostic gateway normalizes completion, tool-calling, streaming, and error handling.
Provider sessions are isolated; a provider switch cannot reuse tokens from a different provider.
Tokens and session references are stored only in OS-backed secure storage; never in plaintext files or env vars.
Expired tokens auto-refresh when possible; if refresh fails, a re-login prompt appears.
Logout removes all local session artifacts and blocks further inference until re-auth.
Median switch time target is under 2 minutes, excluding external consent delays.
userStories.technicalNotes: Reuse /home/spoq/ai-vision/tools/config-gui (Rust/Cargo binary). Store tokens only in OS keychain (keytar or equivalent). Never write credentials to forge-memory.db, .env, or any plaintext file.
safetyGates: No plaintext credentials anywhere on disk
Cross-provider token reuse blocked
Logout verified to clear all session artifacts

userStories.title: Heartbeat Scheduler & Maintenance Playbooks
userStories.description: Implement a configurable heartbeat loop that performs read-only health checks and executes pre-approved maintenance playbooks with full audit trails.
acceptanceCriteria: Heartbeat runs on a 5-minute interval by default; interval is configurable 1–30 minutes via APEX_HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL_SEC.
Default heartbeat checks are read-only: process health, disk pressure (≥85% sustained 5 min), container status, and failed job detection.
Maintenance playbooks are YAML files at ~/.apex/policy/playbooks/{name}.yaml with required fields: name, description, triggers, steps, max_runtime, rollback_commands, rollback_failure_policy.

Trigger thresholds: high_disk_pressure (root fs ≥85% for ≥5 min), service_down (non-zero exit or timeout), memory_pressure (<512 MB available), failed_task (task marked failed with no retry in-flight). Custom triggers may be shell test expressions.
Only playbooks with staging=false execute; staging=true playbooks are queued for operator review and blocked from execution.

Rollback failure handling: if rollback_commands fail, mark the playbook degraded in ~/.apex/state/playbook-health.json, alert the operator, and do not retry until the operator clears the degraded flag.
All heartbeat actions (checks and playbook executions) are audit-logged with timestamp, action, exit code, and output refs.
A failed playbook step is logged but does not halt the heartbeat; execution continues to the next scheduled cycle.
userStories.technicalNotes: Heartbeat runs as a background scheduler (setInterval or equivalent). Playbooks require explicit Canvas/operator approval (staging=false) before heartbeat can execute them. Heartbeat uses a dedicated compact prompt template: ~/.apex/policy/prompts/heartbeat.v{n}.md.
safetyGates: staging=false required before any playbook execution
Rollback failure triggers degraded state and operator alert
All heartbeat actions audit-logged

userStories.title: Checkpoint-based Crash Recovery
userStories.description: Persist task execution state continuously so interrupted work can resume quickly with explicit semantics for interrupted Tier 2 actions.
acceptanceCriteria: Checkpoint schema (versioned JSON) includes: schema_version, task_id, goal, current_step, step_status, pending_approvals, tool_outputs_ref, policy_snapshot_hash, updated_at.
tool_outputs_ref stores a SHA256 hash pointing to output files at ~/.apex/state/outputs/; any output exceeding 10 KB is written to disk and referenced by hash rather than inlined.
Output files are retained for 7 days (configurable via APEX_OUTPUT_RETENTION_DAYS) and cleaned up automatically.
Resumable tool cursors (e.g., line position in log streams, pagination tokens) are stored in the completed step's cursor field so tools resume from their last position on recovery.
On crash recovery: restore the latest checkpoint, mark all in-flight steps as interrupted, and require re-approval for any interrupted Tier 2 actions before resuming.
Target MTTR is under 10 seconds from process restart to resumed task execution.
Non-recoverable state (subprocess handles, network sockets, timer handlers) is explicitly reset on recovery without attempting to restore it.
userStories.technicalNotes: Store checkpoints as versioned JSON in ~/.apex/state/. Hash tool outputs with SHA256 and write large outputs to ~/.apex/state/outputs/. Checkpoint recovery code must resolve hash → file path and read content on demand. See PRD §7.2–7.3 for full schema and cursor spec.
safetyGates: SHA256 output hash verification on recovery
Tier 2 re-approval enforced after any crash interruption

userStories.title: Memory Model & Context Budgeting
userStories.description: Implement three-tier memory (short-term, episodic, durable) with deterministic context budget enforcement and model-size-aware allocation.
acceptanceCriteria: Three memory tiers: short-term (active task only), episodic (30-day TTL from last access, 50 MB quota per workspace, LRU eviction on overflow), durable (explicit user approval required for promotion).

Durable promotion requires all three: usefulness across at least 2 independent sessions, confidence score ≥0.8 (calculated as positive_feedback_count / total_retrievals per session from explicit user thumbs-up/down only), and explicit user approval.
Context budget partition defaults: 25% system policy, 35% active task state, 25% recent actions, 15% retrieved memory.
Large models (≥100K tokens): use default 25/35/25/15 split.
Medium models (16K–100K tokens): scale total usable budget by ×0.75; percentages remain 25/35/25/15.
Small models (<16K tokens): scale by ×0.50; minimum floors enforced — system_policy never below 10%, active_task never below 15%.
On context limit breach: truncate in order — retrieved_memory (oldest first) → recent_actions (oldest first) → active_task_state (lowest relevance) → system_policy (never truncated).
Large tool outputs are chunked: retain first 500 and last 500 tokens in prompt context; store raw output at ~/.apex/state/outputs/ referenced by SHA256 hash.
Budget percentages are user-configurable per model profile in ~/.apex/config/model-profiles.json.
userStories.technicalNotes: Confidence scoring is user-feedback-driven only — never LLM-determined, to ensure determinism and auditability. Episodic memory items become durable promotion candidates after meeting all three criteria; candidates are surfaced for explicit operator approval. See PRD §8.1–8.2 for full memory review UX spec (Phase 2 UI; Phase 1 implements the data layer and promotion logic only).
safetyGates: Durable promotion requires explicit user approval — no auto-promotion
system_policy context segment is never truncated

userStories.title: Runtime Wiring & Interactive REPL
userStories.description: Wire all Phase 1 modules into a unified ApexRuntime and expose an interactive REPL for user testing. The REPL is the Phase 1 user interface — it lets operators exercise shell execution, Docker ops, and policy enforcement interactively before Canvas UX is built in Phase 2.
acceptanceCriteria: ApexRuntime class instantiates and wires all Phase 1 services: ShellEngine, DockerEngine, TieredFirewall, PolicyFileOps, AuthManager, HeartbeatScheduler, CheckpointStore, MemoryManager, AuditLog.
ApexRuntime exposes start() and stop() lifecycle methods; stop() gracefully shuts down the heartbeat, reaps active shell/Docker processes, and flushes audit logs.

REPL supports these commands: `run <shell command>` (shell execution), `docker <list|start|stop|inspect> [args]` (Docker ops), `status` (show heartbeat status, active processes, memory stats), `help` (list commands), `exit` (graceful shutdown).
Tier 1 actions execute immediately and print result with exit code and stdout/stderr.
Tier 2 actions pause and print an approval prompt: `[TIER 2] <action> — Approve? (y/n):`. On y, execute and print result. On n, print 'Denied.' and return to prompt.
Tier 3 actions are immediately rejected with `[TIER 3 BLOCKED] <reason>` and return to prompt — no approval prompt shown.
Output streams incrementally to the REPL as chunks arrive (not buffered until completion).
Startup sequence: init runtime → check ~/.apex/ directories exist (create if missing) → print banner with version, active provider, heartbeat interval → drop into REPL prompt.
A `npm run apex` script starts the REPL via ts-node.
All REPL interactions are audit-logged with tier, action, and outcome.
userStories.technicalNotes: ApexRuntime lives at src/apex/runtime/ApexRuntime.ts. REPL lives at src/apex/repl/Repl.ts using Node.js readline. Wire TieredFirewall.onApprovalRequired callback to the REPL's stdin prompt for Tier 2 approvals. ShellEngine and DockerEngine already accept IPolicyFirewall — pass the TieredFirewall instance. Entry point: src/apex/main.ts, run via ts-node.
safetyGates: Tier 3 actions never prompt for approval — immediate block only
All REPL commands audit-logged before execution

userStories.title: Heartbeat-driven Context Awareness
userStories.description: Extend the heartbeat from a health monitor into a continuous context accumulator. Every pulse snapshots the environment, computes deltas, classifies changes, and writes observations into episodic memory. Sessions open with ambient awareness of what happened while Apex was not in conversation. Soul.md and Behavior.md are loaded at every session start as the agent's identity and operational ground truth.
acceptanceCriteria: Each heartbeat pulse produces an EnvironmentSnapshot: running processes, container states, disk usage per mount, available memory, and active network connections.
Delta analysis compares current snapshot to the previous one and classifies each change as routine, notable, or urgent per the thresholds defined in Behavior.md.

Routine changes are written to the audit log only. Notable changes are written to episodic memory with timestamp, delta description, and classification. Urgent changes are posted to the agent message bus immediately.

After every N pulses (default 12, configurable — approx 1 hour at 5-min interval), accumulated notable deltas are summarized into a human-readable heartbeat narrative and stored as a durable context entry under key 'heartbeat_narrative'.

ApexRuntime.start() loads Soul.md and Behavior.md from src/apex/ into working memory before the REPL opens. If either file is missing, Apex logs a warning and continues — they are identity documents, not hard dependencies.
Session start reads the latest heartbeat_narrative from durable context and prints a brief ambient status to the user before the first prompt: what changed, what is urgent, what is healthy.
Urgent threshold definitions match Behavior.md: mandatory service down, disk >= 85%, available memory < 512 MB, anomalous CPU/memory on any process.
All snapshot data and delta classifications are written through the existing EpisodicStore and AuditLog — no new persistence layer introduced.
userStories.technicalNotes: EnvironmentSnapshot lives at src/apex/heartbeat/EnvironmentSnapshot.ts. Delta analysis at src/apex/heartbeat/DeltaAnalyzer.ts. Narrative summarization calls ProviderGateway (JAL-005) with a compact prompt — if no provider is configured, summarize deterministically from delta counts. Soul.md and Behavior.md are loaded as plain text into ApexRuntime working memory — they are the agent's identity layer, not config files.
safetyGates: Snapshot collection is read-only — no writes to system state
Narrative summarization never includes credentials, tokens, or raw command output

userStories.title: Natural Language Goal Loop
userStories.description: Wire ProviderGateway into a plan-execute-observe agent loop. Kirk states a goal in natural language. Apex decomposes it into steps, classifies each through TieredFirewall, executes with streaming output, self-corrects on failure, and reports completion. This is the core of what makes Apex an agent rather than a shell wrapper.
acceptanceCriteria: REPL accepts `goal <natural language>` command and routes it through the goal loop.

Goal loop prompt includes: Soul.md identity, Behavior.md operational rules, current heartbeat narrative, available tools (shell, docker, file ops), the stated goal, and outputs of prior steps in the current loop.
Apex decomposes the goal into an ordered list of concrete steps and executes them sequentially, streaming output to the REPL as each step runs.

Each step is classified through TieredFirewall before execution. Tier 1 runs immediately. Tier 2 pauses the loop, prompts for approval, and resumes or aborts based on response. Tier 3 aborts the loop with a clear explanation.
On step failure, Apex retries up to 2 times with the error output included in the LLM context. After 2 failures, Apex reports what it tried, what failed, and what it recommends — then stops.
Each completed goal loop writes an execution trace to episodic memory: goal, steps attempted, outcomes, and any errors.
Goal loop state is checkpointed after each completed step so a crash can resume from the last successful step.
On completion, Apex prints a plain-English summary: what was done, what changed, anything Kirk should know.
userStories.technicalNotes: GoalLoop lives at src/apex/agent/GoalLoop.ts. Uses ProviderGateway (JAL-005) for LLM calls. Reads Soul.md and Behavior.md from ApexRuntime working memory — do not re-read from disk. Steps are typed as GoalStep: { id, description, command, tool, tier, status, output, error }. Checkpoint each step via CheckpointStore (JAL-007). Write execution trace via EpisodicStore (JAL-008).
safetyGates: Every step classified through TieredFirewall before execution — no bypass
Tier 3 always aborts the goal loop — never prompts
Credentials and tokens never included in LLM prompt context

userStories.title: Expanded Tool Catalog
userStories.description: Extend Apex's built-in tool set beyond shell and Docker so the goal loop has richer primitives to work with. All tools flow through TieredFirewall and produce structured results the LLM can reason about.
acceptanceCriteria: File tools: read <path>, write <path> <content>, list <dir>, search <pattern> <dir> (grep), diff <path1> <path2>. All subject to PolicyFileOps tier classification.
Process tools: ps (list all), kill <pid> (Tier 2), top-n <n> (top N by CPU). Read-only ops are Tier 1; kill is Tier 2.
Network tools: ping <host>, port-check <host> <port>, curl <url> (GET only, Tier 1). No write network ops in Phase 2.
Log tools: tail <path> <lines>, log-grep <pattern> <path>. Both Tier 1 read-only.
System tools: env (environment variables, redacted for secrets), uptime, df (disk), free (memory), which <binary>.
All tools return a structured ToolResult: { tool, args, tier, exit_code, stdout, stderr, duration_ms } compatible with GoalLoop step output.
GoalLoop prompt includes the tool catalog so the LLM knows what is available.
All tool invocations are audit-logged with tool name, args, tier decision, and outcome.
userStories.technicalNotes: Tool catalog lives at src/apex/tools/. Each tool implements ITool: { name, description, tier, execute(args): Promise<ToolResult> }. ToolRegistry at src/apex/tools/ToolRegistry.ts registers all tools and exposes catalog() for LLM context injection. Tools delegate execution to ShellEngine or PolicyFileOps — never spawn processes directly.
safetyGates: curl restricted to GET — no POST/PUT/DELETE in Phase 2
env output redacts values matching secret patterns (token, key, password, secret)
kill always Tier 2 regardless of PID

userStories.title: Canvas Backend
userStories.description: Build the real-time event server that Canvas frontend will connect to. Streams system status, command output, heartbeat pulses, and approval requests over WebSocket. Exposes a REST API for task history, memory inspection, and policy management.
acceptanceCriteria: WebSocket server starts on configurable port (default 7474) when ApexRuntime starts. Clients connect and immediately receive a full system snapshot as the first message.

Event types streamed to Canvas: system.status (CPU, memory, disk, containers), command.output (incremental chunks), approval.requested (Tier 2 pause), approval.resolved, heartbeat.pulse, task.started, task.completed, task.failed.

REST API endpoints: GET /status (current system state), GET /tasks (recent task history), GET /memory/episodic (recent episodic entries), GET /memory/durable (durable context), GET /policy/allowlist, POST /approvals/:id/approve, POST /approvals/:id/deny.
All WebSocket events use the outbound envelope from PRD §14.2: event_id, event_type, task_id, tier, created_at, payload.
WebSocket connections are authenticated via a session token generated at ApexRuntime start and printed to the REPL on startup.
Canvas server binds to localhost only by default — not exposed to network without explicit config.
All approval POST endpoints require the session token in Authorization header.
userStories.technicalNotes: Canvas backend at src/apex/canvas/CanvasServer.ts using Node.js http + ws (WebSocket library). REST routes at src/apex/canvas/routes/. EventBus at src/apex/canvas/EventBus.ts — ApexRuntime publishes to EventBus; CanvasServer subscribes and fans out to connected clients. Bind to 127.0.0.1 only.
safetyGates: Binds to 127.0.0.1 only — never 0.0.0.0 without explicit opt-in
Session token required for all approval endpoints
No credentials or raw secrets in any streamed event payload

userStories.title: Canvas Frontend
userStories.description: Build the real-time operational dashboard that connects to the Canvas backend. Shows live system state, terminal output, heartbeat status, approval surfaces, and memory panel. Built with React + Vite, served by the Canvas backend.
acceptanceCriteria: React + Vite app served as static files by CanvasServer at GET /canvas. Opens in browser at http://localhost:7474/canvas.
System dashboard panel: live CPU, RAM, disk usage bars, container list with status badges. Updates on every system.status event.
Terminal mirror panel: displays incremental command output chunks as they arrive via command.output events. Scrolls automatically. Preserves last 500 lines.

Approval surface: when approval.requested event arrives, a modal appears showing action description, risk reason, tier, and Approve / Deny buttons. Buttons POST to /approvals/:id/approve or /approvals/:id/deny with session token.
Heartbeat panel: shows last pulse timestamp, last narrative summary, and a timeline of recent notable/urgent events.
Memory panel: lists recent episodic entries (last 20) and all durable context keys with values. Read-only in Phase 2.
Connection status indicator: shows connected/reconnecting/disconnected. Auto-reconnects with exponential backoff on disconnect.
No external UI library dependencies beyond React and Vite — keep the bundle minimal.
userStories.technicalNotes: Canvas frontend at src/apex/canvas/ui/. Built with Vite, output to src/apex/canvas/ui/dist/ which CanvasServer serves statically. WebSocket connects to ws://localhost:7474/ws with session token as query param. State managed with React useState/useReducer — no Redux. Components: SystemDashboard, TerminalMirror, ApprovalModal, HeartbeatPanel, MemoryPanel.
safetyGates: Session token sent as query param on WebSocket connect — never in localStorage
Approval buttons disabled after first click to prevent double-submit

userStories.title: Reasoning & Context Optimization
userStories.description: Make Apex smarter about what context it includes in LLM calls. Relevance-score episodic memory retrieval, optimize context packing against the budget model, and summarize long task histories so goal loops stay coherent across complex multi-step operations.
acceptanceCriteria: Episodic memory retrieval ranks entries by relevance to the current goal using keyword overlap and recency score (recency weight 0.4, keyword overlap weight 0.6). Top-K entries (default K=10) are included in goal loop context.

Context packing respects the ContextBudget model (JAL-008): system_policy 25%, active_task 35%, recent_actions 25%, retrieved_memory 15%. If any segment exceeds its allocation, truncate per the defined order.

Goal loop task histories longer than 2000 tokens are summarized via ProviderGateway before being included in the next LLM call. Summary replaces raw history in the prompt; raw history is retained in episodic memory.
GoalLoop tracks token usage per step and logs a warning when approaching 80% of the model's context limit.
Relevance scorer is deterministic and unit-testable — not LLM-driven. Uses term frequency against a keyword index built from the goal statement.
All context optimization decisions are logged at debug level with segment sizes and truncation actions taken.
userStories.technicalNotes: RelevanceScorer at src/apex/memory/RelevanceScorer.ts. ContextPacker at src/apex/memory/ContextPacker.ts — wraps existing ContextBudget. Summarizer at src/apex/agent/Summarizer.ts — calls ProviderGateway with a compact summarization prompt. Wire into GoalLoop at context assembly time.
safetyGates: Relevance scorer never includes durable memory entries marked sensitive
Summarizer never includes credentials, tokens, or raw secrets in prompt

userStories.title: Slack & Telegram Plugin Integration
userStories.description: Implement the Phase 2 collaboration plugin layer per PRD §14. Apex can send alerts, task summaries, and approval requests to Slack and Telegram. Operators can approve or deny Tier 2 actions from chat. Uses polling mode by default — no inbound webhooks required.
acceptanceCriteria: Plugin coordinator runs as a local service inside ApexRuntime. Maintains per-workspace message queues at ~/.apex/state/plugin-queues/. Exposes GET /apex/plugin-actions/:workspace_id and POST /apex/plugin-actions/:workspace_id/ack/:action_id.
Outbound events use the §14.2 envelope: event_id (UUID), event_type, workspace_id, task_id, tier, created_at, payload, redaction_level, signature (HMAC-SHA256).
Slack plugin: sends approval.requested as an interactive message with Approve/Deny buttons. Polls coordinator for inbound actions every 10 seconds. Bot scopes: chat:write, interactions:read.
Telegram plugin: sends approval.requested as a message with inline keyboard buttons. Same polling contract as Slack.
Approval tokens are single-use, 10-minute TTL, bound to workspace_id + approval_id. Tokens are invalidated on first use or expiry.
Actor identity mapping: ~/.apex/config/plugin-actors.json maps Slack/Telegram actor IDs to local Apex identities. Unmapped actors are rejected and audit-logged.
Plugin disconnect: in-flight approval tokens are immediately expired. Canvas reverts to HITL-only mode and notifies operator.
Redaction level standard applied by default: omits code snippets, file paths, env var names. Only task goal, risk class, and approval chain visible in chat.
userStories.technicalNotes: Plugin coordinator at src/apex/plugins/PluginCoordinator.ts. Slack adapter at src/apex/plugins/SlackPlugin.ts. Telegram adapter at src/apex/plugins/TelegramPlugin.ts. Both implement IPlugin: { send(event), poll(): InboundAction[] }. Bot tokens stored in OS keychain via SecretToolKeychain — never in config files. See PRD §14 for full contract.
safetyGates: HMAC-SHA256 signature verified on every inbound action
Unmapped actors always rejected — no fallback
Bot tokens stored in OS keychain only — never written to disk or logged
